category_summary:
The AI community was dominated by several major threads on February 24th. **Andrej Karpathy** went massively viral [arguing CLIs are ideal](/?date=2026-02-25&category=social#item-176a37dd4c96) for AI agents, urging companies to build agent-accessible surfaces as a "legacy" technology renaissance.

- **NVIDIA**'s Jim Fan [unveiled **SONIC**](/?date=2026-02-25&category=social#item-5f0fb98200f1), a 42M-parameter transformer for humanoid robot whole-body control with zero-shot sim-to-real transfer — a significant open-source robotics milestone
- **Anthropic** [released **RSP v3.0**](/?date=2026-02-25&category=social#item-932e7a56e280), separating unilateral safety commitments from industry recommendations and pledging new transparency via Frontier Safety Roadmaps, amid a reported **Pentagon** ultimatum demanding military access to Claude
- **François Chollet** drove an influential multi-post thread [arguing AI will expand demand](/?date=2026-02-25&category=social#item-7117b375cbba) via Jevons paradox, not eliminate engineering jobs, while also [pushing back on fatalism](/?date=2026-02-25&category=social#item-f6ec47222114) around AI adoption
- **Neel Nanda** [shared interpretability research](/?date=2026-02-25&category=social#item-d515cff14351) showing LLMs implicitly plan ahead when writing poetry, while **Yudkowsky** [proposed banning AI impersonation](/?date=2026-02-25&category=social#item-4b73ef3c2cae) of humans worldwide
- **Ethan Mollick** [highlighted research](/?date=2026-02-25&category=social#item-89bbb65da63d) showing AI narrows skill gaps by 75% between education levels, and [predicted human interaction](/?date=2026-02-25&category=social#item-e11d9ef9c0b6) will retreat to invite-only spaces as AI floods the open web
category_summary_html:
The AI community was dominated by several major threads on February 24th. Andrej Karpathy went massively viral arguing CLIs are ideal for AI agents, urging companies to build agent-accessible surfaces as a "legacy" technology renaissance.

  • NVIDIA's Jim Fan unveiled SONIC, a 42M-parameter transformer for humanoid robot whole-body control with zero-shot sim-to-real transfer — a significant open-source robotics milestone
  • Anthropic released RSP v3.0, separating unilateral safety commitments from industry recommendations and pledging new transparency via Frontier Safety Roadmaps, amid a reported Pentagon ultimatum demanding military access to Claude
  • François Chollet drove an influential multi-post thread arguing AI will expand demand via Jevons paradox, not eliminate engineering jobs, while also pushing back on fatalism around AI adoption
  • Neel Nanda shared interpretability research showing LLMs implicitly plan ahead when writing poetry, while Yudkowsky proposed banning AI impersonation of humans worldwide
  • Ethan Mollick highlighted research showing AI narrows skill gaps by 75% between education levels, and predicted human interaction will retreat to invite-only spaces as AI floods the open web

themes.name: Build for Agents / CLI Renaissance
themes.description: Karpathy's viral thesis that companies should build agent-accessible interfaces (CLIs, MCP, Skills, markdown docs), with agents as first-class users of products and services.

themes.name: NVIDIA SONIC Humanoid Robot Control
themes.description: Major open-source release from NVIDIA: a 42M parameter transformer achieving zero-shot sim-to-real transfer for diverse humanoid robot whole-body control tasks.

themes.name: Anthropic RSP v3.0 & Safety Policy
themes.description: Anthropic releases version 3.0 of its Responsible Scaling Policy with new transparency commitments, separated unilateral vs industry commitments, and mandatory Frontier Safety Roadmaps and Risk Reports

themes.name: Pentagon-Anthropic Governance Crisis
themes.description: Reports of a Pentagon ultimatum to Anthropic giving until Friday to grant military unfettered access to Claude, with threat of Defense Production Act. Described as the most consequential AI governance moment in years.

themes.name: Software Engineering Jobs & Jevons Paradox
themes.description: Chollet's influential thread arguing that AI making software cheaper to produce will expand total demand for software and engineers, not eliminate them — citing Jevons paradox and the need for foundational skills.

themes.name: Technological Determinism vs Human Agency
themes.description: Chollet's pushback against the 'inevitable progress' narrative, arguing society has collective duty to shape AI adoption for human benefit rather than surrendering agency.

themes.name: AI Bots Degrading Social Media
themes.description: Emollick's observations about AI-generated comments flooding social media, eroding human interaction, and predicting migration to invite-only spaces (Discords, group chats).

themes.name: AI Regulation & Governance
themes.description: Yudkowsky proposes AI impersonation bans as a 'trial balloon' for building governance capacity; Anthropic's RSP updates; broader discussion of proactive regulation before catastrophic AI misuse

themes.name: Mechanistic Interpretability
themes.description: Neel Nanda shares research on implicit planning in LLMs during poetry generation using steering vectors; Anthropic interpretability team hiring

themes.name: Claude Code & Anthropic Tooling
themes.description: Multiple announcements about Claude Code ecosystem: Slack plugin integration, Chrome Quick Mode with Opus 4.6, and real-world usage examples of rapid AI-accelerated development.

items.title: CLIs are super exciting precisely because they are a "legacy" technology, which means AI agents can ...
items.content:
CLIs are super exciting precisely because they are a "legacy" technology, which means AI agents can natively and easily use them, combine them, interact with them via the entire terminal toolkit.

E.g ask your Claude/Codex agent to install this new Polymarket CLI and ask for any arbitrary dashboards or interfaces or logic. The agents will build it for you. Install the Github CLI too and you can ask them to navigate the repo, see issues, PRs, discussions, even the code itself.

Example: Claude built this terminal dashboard in ~3 minutes, of the highest volume polymarkets and the 24hr change. Or you can make it a web app or whatever you want. Even more powerful when you use it as a module of bigger pipelines.

If you have any kind of product or service think: can agents access and use them?

- are your legacy docs (for humans) at least exportable in markdown?
- have you written Skills for your product?
- can your product/service be usable via CLI? Or MCP?
- ...

It's 2026. Build. For. Agents.

items.title: What can half of GPT-1 do? We trained a 42M transformer called SONIC to control the body of a humano...
items.content:
What can half of GPT-1 do? We trained a 42M transformer called SONIC to control the body of a humanoid robot. It takes a remarkable amount of subconscious processing for us humans to squat, turn, crawl, sprint. SONIC captures this "System 1" - the fast, reactive whole-body intelligence - in a single model that translates any motion command into stable, natural motor signals. And it's all open-source!! 

The key insight: motion tracking is the one, true scalable task for whole body control. Instead of hand-engineering rewards for every new skill, we use dense, frame-by-frame supervision from human mocap data. The data itself encodes the reward function: "configure your limbs in any human-like position while maintaining balance".

We scaled humanoid motion RL to an unprecedented scale: 100M+ mocap frames and 500,000+ parallel robots across 128 GPUs. NVIDIA Isaac Lab allows us to accelerate physics at 10,000x faster tick, giving robots many years of virtual experience in only hours of wall clock time. After 3 days of training, the neural net transfers zero-shot to the real G1 robot with no finetuning. 100% success rate across 50 diverse real-world motion sequences. 

One SONIC policy supports all of the following:

- VR whole-body teleoperation
- Human video. Just point a webcam to live stream motions.
- Text prompts. "Walk sideways", "dance like a monkey", "kick your left foot", etc.
- Music audio. The robot dances to the beat, adapting to tempo and rhythm.
- VLA foundation models. We plugged in GR00T N1.5 and achieved 95% success on mobile tasks.

We open-source the code and model checkpoints!! Deep dive in thread:
items.author: @DrJimFan
items.summary: NVIDIA's Jim Fan announces SONIC, a 42M parameter transformer for humanoid robot whole-body control. Trained on 100M+ mocap frames across 128 GPUs using Isaac Lab. Zero-shot transfer to real G1 robot with 100% success rate. Supports VR teleoperation, video imitation, text prompts, music-driven dance, and VLA foundation models (GR00T N1.5). Fully open-source.
items.reasoning: Major open-source robotics research release from NVIDIA. Very high engagement (935 likes, 110K views). Technically significant: tiny model achieving zero-shot sim-to-real transfer for diverse humanoid tasks. Open-source weights and code.

items.title:
We're updating our Responsible Scaling Policy to its third version.

Since it came into effect in 20...
items.content:
We're updating our Responsible Scaling Policy to its third version.

Since it came into effect in 2023, we’ve learned a lot about the RSP’s benefits and its shortcomings. This update improves the policy, reinforcing what worked and committing us to even greater transparency.

items.title: A lot of the current discourse about AI comes from a fatalistic position of total surrender of agenc...
items.content:
A lot of the current discourse about AI comes from a fatalistic position of total surrender of agency: "tech is moving in this direction and there's nothing anyone can do about it" (suspiciously convenient for those who stand to benefit most)

But in a free society, we get to choose what kind of world we live in, independent of technological capabilities. Just because tetraethyllead made engines run more efficiently and saved money didn't mean we were *obligated* to pump it into the lungs of our kids

Technological determinism is BS. We have a collective duty to make sure AI adoption improves the human condition, rather than hollows it out
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: Chollet argues against technological determinism in AI adoption, comparing it to leaded gasoline. States society has a duty to ensure AI improves rather than hollows out the human condition.
items.reasoning: Powerful, well-articulated stance from a major AI figure. Very high engagement (1,228 likes, 38K views). Challenges prevailing 'inevitable progress' narrative with historical analogy.

items.title: New paper on implicit planning on LLMs: When writing poetry, LLMs know the sound the current line sh...
items.content:
New paper on implicit planning on LLMs: When writing poetry, LLMs know the sound the current line should end in, choose words to set it up, and you can change the sound

The result isn't new, but we did it with the simple method of steering vectors, so we could study many models
items.author: @NeelNanda5
items.summary: Neel Nanda shares new paper on implicit planning in LLMs: when writing poetry, LLMs know the end-sound of a line in advance and choose words to set it up. Used steering vectors to study this across many models.
items.reasoning: Significant interpretability research from a leading mechanistic interpretability researcher (formerly DeepMind, now Anthropic). Demonstrates LLMs do implicit lookahead planning during generation. 224 likes, technically novel method application.
items.summary_html: Neel Nanda shares new paper on implicit planning in LLMs: when writing poetry, LLMs know the end-sound of a line in advance and choose words to set it up. Used steering vectors to study this across many models.

items.title:
I'm with Vitalik. Anthropic will win a lot of fans if it does not back down.

As part of my work wit...
items.content:
I'm with Vitalik. Anthropic will win a lot of fans if it does not back down.

As part of my work with @blevlabs I had it run on all AI posts here on X today and had it write me a short essay on the AI news of the day, and the fight between the Pentagon and Anthropic is news number one. 

Present that here, the rest of this post is written by AI:

+++++++++++

Today is not a normal day in AI. 

I read hundreds of thousands of posts across 15 curated lists this morning and one signal is unmistakable: multiple technological and geopolitical inflection points converged simultaneously. 

Here is what actually matters.

The governance crisis is real and the deadline is Friday. 

The US Pentagon gave Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei until Friday night to grant military unfettered access to Claude — or face the Defense Production Act, a wartime industrial law that would compel Anthropic to train a military-grade "WarClaude." Claude is the ONLY AI on classified DoD systems. 

Every other major lab — OpenAI, Google, xAI — agreed to the Pentagon's "all lawful use" standard. Anthropic is the only holdout, citing autonomous drone swarms and mass surveillance concerns. The FT confirmed. 

This is the most consequential AI governance moment since the Manhattan Project. Whatever happens Friday will define whether AI companies can maintain safety principles under government pressure.

++++

We crossed the UX paradigm threshold today. Cursor's new "Demos" feature is the first product where agents send VIDEO of running software instead of code diffs.

 The CEO confirms 1/3 of their PRs are now created autonomously. 

Lee Robinson at Vercel got 621 likes on a single post about it — the most-liked developer tool post I've seen in months. Claude Code Remote Control lets you start on your laptop and transfer to your phone while the agent continues. GPT-5.3-Codex is the first commercial model to have helped build itself. These are not incremental features. The interface between humans and code has permanently changed.

Anthropic is systematically destroying incumbents. They raised $30B at $380B in February (now confirmed: $14B ARR, $2.5B from Claude Code alone). Then spent their credibility on precision market strikes: IBM -13% from COBOL post. Indian IT stocks -6% from Cowork launch. Payment companies repriced from Citrini thesis. Today they added 15+ enterprise connectors and finance plugins covering investment banking, private equity, wealth management, Thomson Reuters, FactSet, S&P Global, MSCI. This is Claude becoming the operating system for all knowledge work.

++++

The open source explosion is real. Six major model launches today: Qwen 3.5 (Flash + 122B + 35B + 27B), MiniMax-M2.5 (Opus-level at 95% cheaper, open source), Mercury 2 (first reasoning diffusion LLM, 5x faster), GPT-5.3-Codex, Gemini 3.1 Pro (77.1% ARC-AGI-2), LFM2-24B (1,132 tok/s on consumer CPU). The cost of frontier intelligence is collapsing to zero.

++++

Physical AI is actually happening now. Waymo expanded to 4 new cities today — Dallas, Houston, San Antonio, Orlando. Figure humanoids run 24/7 at San Jose with no human supervision. XPENG IRON mass production: late 2026. NVIDIA open-sourced DreamDojo: 44,711 hours of robot training video. 164 VLA model submissions at ICLR 2026. The AlphaGo creator raised a $1B seed round. This is not hype anymore.

++++

Watch these specific things: (1) Friday night — Pentagon/Anthropic decision. (2) DeepSeek V4 — imminent, will drop markets. (3) Cursor demos paradigm spreading to other tools. (4) AMD stock — Meta deal gives them 10% via warrants. (5) What Notion launched today — @akothari teased it, details still unclear.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer publishes a comprehensive AI-generated daily news summary covering: Pentagon vs Anthropic ultimatum (Friday deadline for military Claude access), new coding UX paradigms (Cursor Demos, Claude Code Remote), Anthropic's market disruption ($14B ARR, enterprise connectors), open-source model explosion (Qwen 3.5, MiniMax-M2.5, Mercury 2, GPT-5.3-Codex, Gemini 3.1 Pro, LFM2-24B), and physical AI milestones (Waymo expansion, Figure humanoids, XPENG IRON).
items.reasoning: Extremely comprehensive daily AI summary touching virtually every major development. High engagement (102 likes, 24K views). While AI-generated, it aggregates crucial signals: Pentagon/Anthropic confrontation is major governance news, multiple model launches, Anthropic's financial metrics ($14B ARR, $2.5B from Claude Code), and physical AI developments. The Pentagon deadline claim (Friday) is potentially breaking news. However, accuracy of AI-generated summaries should be verified.

items.title: It's probably accelerating from here. More code, more software engineers. More apps, more SaaS usage...
items.content:
It's probably accelerating from here. More code, more software engineers. More apps, more SaaS usage and revenue. More cloud consumption. And a whole lot of tokens through it all.

If the cost of hiring software engineers was previously a bottleneck on software deployment, then making it cheaper to ship code means the entire software ecosystem will start to grow faster.
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: Chollet argues that making it cheaper to ship code will accelerate the entire software ecosystem: more code, more engineers, more apps, more SaaS revenue, more cloud consumption, more tokens.
items.reasoning: Extremely high engagement (1,287 likes, 152K views). Core thesis in Chollet's influential thread. Counternarrative to 'AI replaces developers' with an economic growth argument.
items.summary_html: Chollet argues that making it cheaper to ship code will accelerate the entire software ecosystem: more code, more engineers, more apps, more SaaS revenue, more cloud consumption, more tokens.
items.content_html:
It's probably accelerating from here. More code, more software engineers. More apps, more SaaS usage and revenue. More cloud consumption. And a whole lot of tokens through it all.

If the cost of hiring software engineers was previously a bottleneck on software deployment, then making it cheaper to ship code means the entire software ecosystem will start to grow faster.

items.title:
There are nearly no good reasons for an AI to ever impersonate a human.

Making that illegal in as m...
items.content:
There are nearly no good reasons for an AI to ever impersonate a human.

Making that illegal in as many countries as possible, and improving state capacity to enforce it, would be a good trial balloon for human civilization's ability to ban negative uses of AI.
items.author: @ESYudkowsky
items.summary: Yudkowsky proposes making AI impersonation of humans illegal worldwide as a test of civilization's ability to ban harmful AI uses. 1179 likes.
items.reasoning: High-engagement (1179 likes, 56K views) policy proposal from one of AI safety's most prominent voices. The 'trial balloon' framing is strategic and novel - focusing not just on the specific harm but on building governance capacity. Concrete, actionable proposal.
items.summary_html: Yudkowsky proposes making AI impersonation of humans illegal worldwide as a test of civilization's ability to ban harmful AI uses. 1179 likes.
items.content_html:
There are nearly no good reasons for an AI to ever impersonate a human.

Making that illegal in as many countries as possible, and improving state capacity to enforce it, would be a good trial balloon for human civilization's ability to ban negative uses of AI.

items.title:
How much does distillation really matter for Chinese LLMs?

DeepSeek's usage was a rounding error. M...
items.content:
How much does distillation really matter for Chinese LLMs?

DeepSeek's usage was a rounding error. MiniMax's was substantial. But distillation is getting less important as RL takes over —  it's  easier to access "banned" APIs than to smuggle GPUs.

https://t.co/vbm2ZMVNw4

items.title: I know I keep going on about this, but glance at the comments here to see the future of social media...
items.content:
I know I keep going on about this, but glance at the comments here to see the future of social media: most of the comments are meaning-shaped, most are ultimately nonsense, and because LLMs are good at producing things that feel like they deserve attention, each one is a small tax on your concentration and a drowning out of conversation.

I suspect my posts attract more AI bots than most, but they are coming for you all. Social media is going to either blow up or transform, but until then: don't read the comments.

items.title: The amount of latent demand for software that hasn't been unlocked yet because software is still cos...
items.content:
The amount of latent demand for software that hasn't been unlocked yet because software is still costly to make is orders of magnitude greater than the amount of software that has been deployed so far.

In the limit, everybody could be running a custom-made software suite. Code would be as expendable as words.
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: Chollet argues the latent demand for software that hasn't been built because software is costly is orders of magnitude greater than existing software. In the limit, everyone runs custom software and code becomes as expendable as words.
items.reasoning: Compelling vision of software abundance from Chollet. Strong engagement (256 likes, 20K views). Core argument in the 'will AI kill developer jobs' debate.
items.summary_html: Chollet argues the latent demand for software that hasn't been built because software is costly is orders of magnitude greater than existing software. In the limit, everyone runs custom software and code becomes as expendable as words.
items.content_html:
The amount of latent demand for software that hasn't been unlocked yet because software is still costly to make is orders of magnitude greater than the amount of software that has been deployed so far.

In the limit, everybody could be running a custom-made software suite. Code would be as expendable as words.

items.title: some illuminati somewhere decided today was Launch Everything Day but just sharing some personal com...
items.content:
some illuminati somewhere decided today was Launch Everything Day but just sharing some personal commentary from this as an analyst:

- Scott admits Devin didn’t even have internal PMF at the 2024 launch. took 6 months to get adoption at first enterprise customer. Models werent ready. trying out a lot of agent patterns. But: the form factor was right. Async agents are the Final Boss of agent ux.

- Devin usage doubled every 2 months in 2025 per each enterprise after landing. Doubling rate has *accelerated* to every 6 weeks so far this year (!!) internal usage is now 4x 2025 peak.

- Self serve sucked because repo setup was effectively not worked on (doesnt matter to enterprises if FDEs are just gonna set them up for you lol)

- with Devin 2.2 that is now changing. Cog hired first designer 2 months ago (another lol). team did a crazy allhands 3 Sundays ago and decided to do a big sprint to catch up all the self serve UX debt that has been piling up. this means lots of polish but also integrating things with the new omnibox and seamlessly tying in devin review with devin main to “close the loop”.

point being, this team has been building background agents since long before it was cool and honestly well before they were working. instead of chasing trend after trend in 2025 they found pmf and battle tested for the past year in the largest enterprises in the world - by the way the single most impt customer profile in coding. 

and now allow them to reintroduce themselves with a basically complete reworking of what its like to use Devin. again dont take my word for it - take my designer’s word who has been going absolutely fucking HAM because he now has his own senior engineer reading his figma and translating his vision to code.  see screenshots. I’m feeling the agi myself. 

and…

… look out for Devin 3.0 :)

items.title: In the short term, harm done by impersonating humans is unlikely to be catastrophic, though perhaps ...
items.content:
In the short term, harm done by impersonating humans is unlikely to be catastrophic, though perhaps large.

The reason to enforce that law for real, and with international pursuit of transgressors, would be to increase our collective ability to say "No" to an AI misuse.

items.title:
OmniDocBench is getting saturated 

VLMs are getting increasingly better at document understanding, ...
items.content:
OmniDocBench is getting saturated 

VLMs are getting increasingly better at document understanding, from OSS (DeepSeek-OCR2, GLM-OCR), to frontier (Gemini 3, Kimi 5.2, GPT-5.2). 

A popular benchmark to measure document understanding progress has been OmniDocBench. But we're quickly approaching the point where we need a new benchmark. 

1. The latest models are pushing ~95% on OmniDocBench and are already overfitting the benchmark, while still having real gaps on document capabilities.

2. The evaluation metrics of OmniDocBench depend completely on exact match and not on semantic correctness. The latter is much more important, especially in today's world where LLMs can reason over text tokens regardless of non-important formatting differences.
*(see example below; our own service llamaparse is penalized even though our parsing is 100% semantically correct on scientific notation parsing)* 

There are so many real-world documents that haven't yet been solved by even the latest models. We'd love to welcome discussion on advancing document understanding benchmarks that are more diverse and properly score models on semantic correctness.

Blog: https://t.co/K78WjVCScz
items.author: @jerryjliu0
items.summary: Jerry Liu (LlamaIndex) argues OmniDocBench is becoming saturated at ~95% as VLMs improve, with flawed exact-match metrics missing semantic correctness. Calls for new document understanding benchmarks.
items.reasoning: Substantive technical critique from LlamaIndex founder. Highlights that top models (DeepSeek-OCR2, GLM-OCR, Gemini 3, Kimi 5.2, GPT-5.2) are saturating document understanding benchmarks while real gaps remain. Important discussion about benchmark methodology and semantic vs exact-match evaluation.
items.summary_html: Jerry Liu (LlamaIndex) argues OmniDocBench is becoming saturated at ~95% as VLMs improve, with flawed exact-match metrics missing semantic correctness. Calls for new document understanding benchmarks.
items.content_html:
OmniDocBench is getting saturated

VLMs are getting increasingly better at document understanding, from OSS (DeepSeek-OCR2, GLM-OCR), to frontier (Gemini 3, Kimi 5.2, GPT-5.2).

A popular benchmark to measure document understanding progress has been OmniDocBench. But we're quickly approaching the point where we need a new benchmark.

1. The latest models are pushing ~95% on OmniDocBench and are already overfitting the benchmark, while still having real gaps on document capabilities.

2. The evaluation metrics of OmniDocBench depend completely on exact match and not on semantic correctness. The latter is much more important, especially in today's world where LLMs can reason over text tokens regardless of non-important formatting differences.

*(see example below; our own service llamaparse is penalized even though our parsing is 100% semantically correct on scientific notation parsing)*

There are so many real-world documents that haven't yet been solved by even the latest models. We'd love to welcome discussion on advancing document understanding benchmarks that are more diverse and properly score models on semantic correctness.

Blog: https://t.co/K78WjVCScz

items.title: Here is the thing guys: software was *never* eating the world. Except for a few communication, data ...
items.content: Here is the thing guys: software was *never* eating the world. Except for a few communication, data storage, and word-processing/presentation tools (all of which are 50+ year old technology), the vast majority of business processes hardly changed over the past few decades. I've seen it first hand. Extremely frustrating to realize. That's why turning all of that over to AI now will in the immediate future hardly have much of an impact.
items.author: @tunguz
items.summary: Tunguz argues that 'software eating the world' never really happened — most business processes barely changed in decades. Concludes that AI's near-term business impact will similarly be limited.
items.reasoning: Contrarian and thought-provoking take from a credible VC voice. High engagement (118 likes, 16K views). Challenges mainstream AI hype narrative with real-world observation about enterprise inertia.
items.summary_html: Tunguz argues that 'software eating the world' never really happened — most business processes barely changed in decades. Concludes that AI's near-term business impact will similarly be limited.
items.content_html: Here is the thing guys: software was *never* eating the world. Except for a few communication, data storage, and word-processing/presentation tools (all of which are 50+ year old technology), the vast majority of business processes hardly changed over the past few decades. I've seen it first hand. Extremely frustrating to realize. That's why turning all of that over to AI now will in the immediate future hardly have much of an impact.

items.title: As stories about AI increasingly become stories of either catastrophe or salvation, I worry that peo...
items.content:
As stories about AI increasingly become stories of either catastrophe or salvation, I worry that people are increasingly discounting the possibility (not certainty!) that we get AGI without a singularity.

People are deferring decisions we need to make now for that future. Reminds me of a poem.

items.title: one of the most impressive teams in AI I’ve ever been honored to meet. going to their christmas part...
items.content:
one of the most impressive teams in AI I’ve ever been honored to meet. going to their christmas party last year was a big “oh wow…. they are assembling some low key legends up and down the stack” moment for me. fun fact: when they were fundraising in 2023 NOBODY wanted to touch them because the received wisdom was that the last generation of custom chip startups were abject failures.

congrats on the B! as we run into more and more memory and compute tradeoffs with exponential demand for speed and throughput, i think innovation at this layer makes sense especially as the workload shape stabilizes. The Hardware Lottery doesnt always have to go to the same winners every year…

items.title: @alapshah1 @PmQuant @Citrini7 There is zero demand for an aggregator above the aggregator. Agents ca...
items.content: @alapshah1 @PmQuant @Citrini7 There is zero demand for an aggregator above the aggregator. Agents can just use DoorDash. You can only imagine price, yet somehow cannot understand (1) any of the other functions that DoorDash provides that are essential to market making at scale or (2) why they have a structural advantage in providing those functions. Until you are capable of coming up with a compelling reason why another layer should exist behind (non-existent) price reasons you don’t really have a case
items.author: @benthompson
items.summary: Ben Thompson argues there's zero demand for an aggregator layer above existing aggregators like DoorDash, and agents will just use existing platforms directly
items.reasoning: Insightful strategic commentary from a leading tech analyst about how AI agents interact with aggregator economics. The argument that agents don't create new aggregation layers but use existing ones is important for understanding AI's economic impact.

All I know is I'm shipping features at lightning speed and I'm 100x as fast as I used to be

What that means for me is I am good at making products I think so I can steer things in the right direction fast

Many people are not so they can have this AI that makes them fast but essentially it multiplies trash because they're not good at making products

items.title:
how to build a bootstrapped startup without funding:

1. pick a problem you personally have. if you ...
items.content:
how to build a bootstrapped startup without funding:

1. pick a problem you personally have. if you don't use your own product daily, quit now

2. skip the pitch deck. open your code editor. ship something ugly in a weekend

3. charge money from day 1. free users give you nothing but support tickets

4. use boring tech. PHP, SQLite, vanilla JS. frameworks are a trap that mass waste your time

5. host on cheap VPS ($5-20/mo). not AWS. you don't need kubernetes for 1,000 users

6. do customer support yourself. it's the fastest product feedback loop that exists

7. automate everything you do more than twice. cron jobs > employees.

8. grow on Twitter/X by building in public. your journey IS the marketing

9. keep your burn rate near zero so you never need to raise. ramen profitable > series A

10. say no to investors, cofounders, and "advisors" who want equity for intros

i've been doing this for 10+ years now. no employees, no funding, no board meetings

the entire VC game is designed to make you think you need permission to start

you don't

items.title:
You can use Claude Code to pull live, structured data from any website.

It's a Claude skill.

This ...
items.content:
You can use Claude Code to pull live, structured data from any website.

It's a Claude skill.

This is not about searching the web and returning a wall of text. That sucks.

This skill will return structured data, organized in tables you can work with immediately.

For example:

Ask Claude Code to find every 2-bedroom rental in a specific neighborhood posted in the last 48 hours, extract the price, square footage, and listing URL from multiple sites, and dump everything into a spreadsheet.

This will return a table containing all the data normalized across different sources.

This works using the Nimble skill. It will search the web in real time and return structured output.

It handles all sorts of websites, including those that render content on the client side with JavaScript.

If you build agents that need web data, this skill is a 10/10.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Svpino demonstrates using Claude Code with the Nimble skill to pull live, structured data from websites (e.g., rental listings) and output organized tables. Describes it as a powerful tool for agents needing web data.

This works using the Nimble skill. It will search the web in real time and return structured output.

items.title:
I'm giving instructions to my AI agents at 115wpm.

I can speak almost 2x as fast as I can type now....
items.content:
I'm giving instructions to my AI agents at 115wpm.

I can speak almost 2x as fast as I can type now.

@WisprFlow is the biggest unlock I've found over the last 20 weeks.

I'm trying to replace typing with dictation whenever possible. You can't believe how liberating it is to give instructions to Claude Code almost as fast as you can think about it.

They just released an Android version as well.

Writing code with voice was not on my bingo card, to be honest.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Santiago (svpino) promotes Whispr Flow for voice-based coding, claiming 115 WPM dictation speed to give instructions to Claude Code, calling it a major productivity unlock.

I'm trying to replace typing with dictation whenever possible. You can't believe how liberating it is to give instructions to Claude Code almost as fast as you can think about it.

items.title: @0x_AD7 Remind me which are the two first use cases? I think I talk about compressing the videos fir...

items.summary_html: Svpino discusses using Claude Code to automate personal tasks with MCP tools, and links to a video demonstration.
items.content_html:
@0x_AD7 Remind me which are the two first use cases? I think I talk about compressing the videos first, so I’m not sure whether you are counting that one.

Compressing videos is the one that needs AI the least. I could automate this one with scripts alone, except it was faster to do it with Claude.

Everything else is a better use case for AI because I need to parse emails and check their contents. I could automate some of those steps, but that will make them brittle and dependent on email format changing.

items.title:
The real breakthrough is not graphics quality.

It is persistence and interaction.

These world mode...
items.content:
The real breakthrough is not graphics quality.

It is persistence and interaction.

These world models:

→ Generate environments auto-regressively, frame by frame
→ React continuously to your actions
→ Maintain environmental consistency for minutes
→ Recall previous interactions inside the world

This means the system is simulating a coherent environment, not just predicting the next frame.

That is a fundamental shift.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon discusses the key breakthrough of world models: persistence and interaction, not just graphics quality. Highlights auto-regressive environment generation, continuous reaction to actions, and environmental consistency.
items.reasoning: Provides a technically-grounded analysis of what makes world models fundamentally different from video generation. The persistence/coherence distinction is insightful.
items.summary_html: Ronald van Loon discusses the key breakthrough of world models: persistence and interaction, not just graphics quality. Highlights auto-regressive environment generation, continuous reaction to actions, and environmental consistency.
items.content_html:
The real breakthrough is not graphics quality.

It is persistence and interaction.

These world models:

→ Generate environments auto-regressively, frame by frame

→ React continuously to your actions

→ Maintain environmental consistency for minutes

→ Recall previous interactions inside the world

This means the system is simulating a coherent environment, not just predicting the next frame.

That is a fundamental shift.

items.content:
Then there is “promptable world events.”

Inside the simulation, you can dynamically:

→ Change the weather
→ Modify objects
→ Introduce or alter characters
→ Trigger new scenarios in real time

Think about the enterprise implications:

→ Training embodied agents
→ Autonomous vehicle testing at scale
→ Immersive learning environments

This is a new sandbox for innovation.

Legendary Grammy-winning music producer and artist Wyclef Jean partnered with Google DeepMind and @YouTube to explore Music AI Sandbox, a suite of tools for professional musicians and producers that want to experiment with AI as a collaboration partner.

Watch this behind-the-scenes video to hear Wyclef explore the potential of AI-assisted artistry and discuss how he utilized our tools when creating his song "Back from Abu Dhabi."

items.title:
The first time in X history I think I don't really like the algorithm so much

It seems most posts g...
items.content:
The first time in X history I think I don't really like the algorithm so much

It seems most posts get stuck at 10-50 RTs, and the delay until they grow to even there can take like 24 hours to 3 days!

And I feel like you have to tweet quite extremist now to get views

I liked before more where you could post a regular blog post and personal story and it'd get more views now it barely does
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio criticizes X's current algorithm, noting posts get stuck at 10-50 RTs with 24h-3 day delays, and that you need extremist content to get views. Preferred when personal stories and blog posts got more reach.

items.title:
For our free newsletter this week, we talk about AI from a consumer perspective.

 @IrenaCronin and ...
items.content:
For our free newsletter this week, we talk about AI from a consumer perspective.

 @IrenaCronin and I write this newsletter every week.
 
AI for consumers is about everyday convenience versus risk: people want AI that saves time, works reliably, and stays under their control, but growing use in creation, organization, shopping, and media is raising trust, privacy, and authenticity concerns. 

The shift toward agents that take actions makes clear guardrails, transparency, and easy human escalation essential.

Read for free: https://t.co/HHwYy7NoAl and please subscribe!

As in - is the goal to build a specialised tool that when used by an expert unlocks the super human or is the goal to make the human achievable possible without human input via a generalist AI system.

items.title:
GTC is not just another tech conference.

It’s a preview of what will define enterprise strategy for...
items.content:
GTC is not just another tech conference.

It’s a preview of what will define enterprise strategy for the next 3 to 5 years.

We’re talking about:

→ Generative AI moving into production
→ Multi-agent systems that execute complex workflows
→ Digital twins transforming operations and supply chains
→ Accelerated computing reshaping infrastructure decisions

If you lead AI, data, or transformation, this is signal, not noise.

items.title:
@MSaintjour @blevlabs That is the argument. 

So make it and convince people. I'm skeptical myself. ...
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer discusses tensions between government regulation of private AI companies and American free market values.
items.reasoning: Touches on AI regulation debate but in a conversational, not deeply analytical way.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer discusses tensions between government regulation of private AI companies and American free market values.
items.content_html:
@MSaintjour @blevlabs That is the argument.

So make it and convince people. I'm skeptical myself. American values in the past have been to keep government out of our private corporations and keep it from forcing private companies around.

That said, I think there is a good argument to be made for why that's important to do this time around. And am open to it. But need to be convinced why what you say is true.

items.title:
Lots of talk about AI killing businesses. 

Here is a story about it saving one. 

From reader Adam ...
items.content:
Lots of talk about AI killing businesses. 

Here is a story about it saving one. 

From reader Adam P.:

"AI saved our company. We're two part-timers plus me, a founder of 30 years. We were losing ground to bigger competitors with full marketing teams. SEO stalled, business was flat.

We fed our web data and competitor info into AI. It ran SWOT comparisons, found content gaps, showed where we were being outranked. We restructured our site and tightened our CRM workflows.

Traffic is growing again. Enquiries are stronger. We're back in expansion mode."
items.author: @TheRundownAI
items.summary: AI newsletter shares reader testimonial about AI saving a small business through competitive analysis and SEO optimization
items.reasoning: Anecdotal AI success story. Low substantive value but represents common SMB AI adoption narrative.

items.content:
By the way, I use Wispr Flow in two different languages. 

My overall talking speed is only 115 words per minute because one of those two languages is not my native language, so I speak more slowly. 

I wish I could see how fast I speak in my native language only. I'm sure it's close to 300 wpm :)

items.title:
Learn from Experience! — the key to success in life and in Machine Learning.

"Machine learning is t...
items.content:
Learn from Experience! — the key to success in life and in Machine Learning.

"Machine learning is the study of computer algorithms that automatically improve with experience." — From the first page of Tom Mitchell's book [1997]: https://t.co/d7OkKfkX9R

My paraphrase: "ML is the set of 𝙈𝙖𝙩𝙝𝙚𝙢𝙖𝙩𝙞𝙘𝙖𝙡 𝘼𝙇𝙂𝙊𝙍𝙄𝙏𝙃𝙈𝙎 𝙩𝙝𝙖𝙩 𝙇𝙀𝘼𝙍𝙉 𝙁𝙍𝙊𝙈 𝙀𝙓𝙋𝙀𝙍𝙄𝙀𝙉𝘾𝙀." [𝗺𝘆 𝗲𝗺𝗽𝗵𝗮𝘀𝗶𝘀]

When I read that in 1998, after many years of studying mathematics for physics and astrophysics, it was the first time that I learned about ML, and I asked myself, "𝘞𝘩𝘢𝘵 𝘥𝘰𝘦𝘴 𝘵𝘩𝘢𝘵 𝘮𝘦𝘢𝘯?" Searching for an answer to that question forever changed the trajectory of my career.
items.author: @KirkDBorne
items.summary: Kirk Borne shares personal story of how Tom Mitchell's 1997 ML definition changed his career trajectory
items.reasoning: Personal ML history narrative, mildly interesting but not novel
items.summary_html: Kirk Borne shares personal story of how Tom Mitchell's 1997 ML definition changed his career trajectory

When I read that in 1998, after many years of studying mathematics for physics and astrophysics, it was the first time that I learned about ML, and I asked myself, "𝘞𝘩𝘢𝘵 𝘥𝘰𝘦𝘴 𝘵𝘩𝘢𝘵 𝘮𝘦𝘢𝘯?" Searching for an answer to that question forever changed the trajectory of my career.

items.title:
Fail Fast to Learn Fast!

Failure is information, not identity. 

Failures are data points, not self...
items.content:
Fail Fast to Learn Fast!

Failure is information, not identity. 

Failures are data points, not self-defining deficiencies.

Failure is a calibration, not a catastrophe.

Failure may come when you overestimate the difficulty of the problem or underestimate your own ability. Choose differently. Choose wisely.

Learn now - be fluent later.

Legendary Grammy-winning music producer and artist Wyclef Jean partnered with Google DeepMind and @YouTube to explore Music AI Sandbox, a suite of tools for professional musicians and producers that want to experiment with AI as a collaboration partner.

items.title: I almost never get recognized in Portugal by Portuguese which is odd cause a lot of my audience is f...
items.content:
I almost never get recognized in Portugal by Portuguese which is odd cause a lot of my audience is from there and I live there, I guess cause the Portuguese tech talent left Portugal and just works elsewhere (like US, UK, etc)

But I do get recognized in Brazil a lot in random places like here, so many entrepreneurs and devs here who are also on X, there's so much energy here of people building stuff it's really exciting

This guy looked oddly Portuguese though which was funny

over 5,500 (!!) entries this time￼🤩 and i...
items.content:
meet the winners of the Doki Doki Infinity Imagine Event💘

over 5,500 (!!) entries this time￼🤩 and it was literally impossible to pick just 3... so we made it 5 winners instead (● ˃̶͈̀ロ˂̶͈́)੭ꠥ⁾⁾

huge congrats to our winners - enjoy that $100 prize￼💓

but truly? every entry radiated such main character energy!
the lovely dates. the soft picnics. the chaotic fantasy romances. we’re still blushing, giggling, fully melting over your creations.

thanks for making it this unbearably doki doki 🥰
stay tuned for the next Imagine event. we already know you’re about to go even harder￼.

items.title: Yes. I ran a @bankrbot request a few minutes ago to collect some Ethereum. Wasn't hacked. The money ...
items.content:
Yes. I ran a @bankrbot request a few minutes ago to collect some Ethereum. Wasn't hacked. The money I just received saved my best friend from getting evicted this weekend. 

There is a group creating a token in my name and the money was sitting there. So I requested it to be transfered. 

I feel a little dirty, don't know what I've done, but desperate times require desperate moves.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer describes collecting Ethereum via bankrbot from a token created in his name, using proceeds to help a friend avoid eviction.

items.content_html:
Yes. I ran a @bankrbot request a few minutes ago to collect some Ethereum. Wasn't hacked. The money I just received saved my best friend from getting evicted this weekend.

There is a group creating a token in my name and the money was sitting there. So I requested it to be transfered.

I feel a little dirty, don't know what I've done, but desperate times require desperate moves.

items.title:
did some phosphatidylcholine IV this afternoon

here's how it felt:

- a tiny bit weak at first
- th...
items.content:
did some phosphatidylcholine IV this afternoon

here's how it felt:

- a tiny bit weak at first
- then in 20-30 min felt like I had too much energy that I didn't know how to use
- then stomach felt full
- then felt very warm
- and felt i could use the energy (still working now)
items.author: @TheGregYang
items.summary: Greg Yang (AI researcher) shares personal experience with phosphatidylcholine IV treatment - health/biohacking content
items.reasoning: Off-topic personal health content from an AI researcher, not relevant to AI/ML
items.summary_html: Greg Yang (AI researcher) shares personal experience with phosphatidylcholine IV treatment - health/biohacking content
items.content_html:
did some phosphatidylcholine IV this afternoon

here's how it felt:

  • a tiny bit weak at first
  • then in 20-30 min felt like I had too much energy that I didn't know how to use
  • then stomach felt full
  • then felt very warm
  • and felt i could use the energy (still working now)

items.title:
🇧🇷 Waking up in Brazil near the ocean

South Brazil has lots of great villas available although defi...
items.content:
🇧🇷 Waking up in Brazil near the ocean

South Brazil has lots of great villas available although definitely not at the price point of South East Asia, I'd say 3-5x more expensive and whether that's worth it for you is debatable

Build quality of villas in Brazil honestly really varies, a lot of it sucks and is cheap, but a lot of it is built with love and great materials too, kinda hit and miss, it depends if the owner is rich and built it for themselves or not

Brazil is also a great place to build a villa, lots of land for sale, approval is fast, and there's no shortage of workers to build it (like in Europe)

Brazil also has lots of great architects and interior designers, and they just make beautiful stuff

This villa I love because it has that Bali white glossy stone (I think it's boho chic) and it's just great to walk down on barefeet, also I love the rotating stairs and this little jungle inside the living room

Then you go outside and you hear the sea! I love living near the sea and I think the salty air really is healthy for you

Only bad thing which is common here: no AC in living rooms, only in bedrooms. Why? People like to get the natural wind to cool although at peak summer (like February) it gets boiling hot

Oh last thing many villas in Brazil come with a chef included (kinda like Bali and I think there's a colonial connection here as in Bali you sometimes get a "babu" and in Brazil it's called "babá")

They cook for you what you want and get groceries etc

Nice!

items.title: I think increasingly it's just because UK and W-Europe is getting exponentially worse while exponent...
items.content:
I think increasingly it's just because UK and W-Europe is getting exponentially worse while exponentially increasing taxes

Like if everything around you turns into a shithole, then you fly to Dubai or Asia and it's just refreshing to finally be able to walk around in a clean shopping mall with shiny floors where the staff is nice to you and the streets aren't littered with trash and unsafe with thugs trying to stab or rob you

It's not even that Dubai or Asia is so great, it's that what's left of W-Europe isn't so great anymore for the amount in cost of living and taxes it costs to live there!
